comments.title: Magical Ethics
comments.content: I'm thinking of doing a protection spell for my niece and nephew (both minors).  I'm their godfather in the RCC.  They and their mother (my sister) live with me.  Their father died a few years ago and so I've had to step into the godfather role in a full-time way.  I'm not a legal guardian, though.  I have no doubt my sister would consent to me doing a magical spell to protect them but I'm wondering, do magical ethics require it?  As far as the RCC goes, I can give them blessings and perform a role similar to what their father would have.

replies.title: Re: Magical Ethics
replies.content: I don't happen to know what rights a godparent has in Catholic tradition. Since you're sure that your sister would consent, though, why not ask her?

replies.title: Re: Magical Ethics
replies.content:
I will ask. The question was more of an academic one, since I'm still learning about magic, including the ethical aspects of it.

1. Would asking my sister for a general permission to perform spells for my godchildren be ethically acceptable? Mostly, my sister doesn't believe in magic but respects that I do. Repeatedly asking her permission to do things she considers silly is a bit awkward for me, and I'm a rather private person. That would be the second reason I asked.

2. What kind of rights would a godparent in the RCC need in order to be able to perform spells, on their own, on behalf of their godchildren?

replies.title: Re: Magical Ethics
replies.content:
1) Of course! If she's comfortable giving you blanket permission, that's all you need.

2) Any formal permission to make religious commitments on behalf of the child.

replies.title: Re: Magical Ethics
replies.content:
Per definition (in RCC tradition) a baptismal Godfather is the designated male figure to take on religious instruction of the child, shall the father becomes unable to fulfill this responsibility due to death or grave injury. Other sacraments do bring their own Godfather figures, which are closer to a mentor/role model (e.g. RCC weddings include the figure of marital Godparents, who ough to be an older, married, couple that should help and advice the newly weds in an impartial way, since their natural parents are most likely to take sides).

Now, the spirit of the oath is not "to raise them as if my own children", but "to raise them, to the best of my ability, as their father/mother would have done". The letter of the oath is to raise them as "good Christians" (i.e. Roman Catholics). Depending on the exact wording of the ritual, there might be some other restrictions as well.

Regarding the original question, it is my opinion that you should consider (to the best of your ability): 1) Is this sort of magic one I would comfortably perform and still call myself an observant Catholic? and 2) Would the deceased parent approve?

replies.title: Re: Magical Ethics
replies.content:
1) Yes, I have no doubts that my magical practices are line with being an observant Catholic.
2) Yes, he would have. My late brother-in-law and I were closer than I am to my own brother, and I can say he wouldn't have the slightest issue with this.

replies.title: Re: Magical Ethics
replies.content:
There you go, then. Not that I expected less of you, or that I claimed the right to judge you, specifically. But since we are talking about the theoretical boundaries, I wanted to point out that it is not only the Christian God we have duties towards, but to the Faithful Departed as well.

Bless you, brother!

replies.title: Re: Magical Ethics
replies.content: Thank you, and bless you as well!

replies.title: Re: Magical Ethics
replies.content: Another question: I have several family members that I have damaged relationships with.  I'm trying to repair those, and have made overtures that are promising.  I would like do a spell with Santa Muerte to help me continue fixing those relationships.  Santa Muerte will work with anyone for most any reason, so the spells to this end are not always within the ethical consent requirement you've voiced multiple times.  Would it be possible to devise a general spell for myself to the end of helping me work on these relationships, without directly bringing magical power down onto these family members themselves?  Like a general spell to make me a better family member, more understanding, patient, etc., but obviously with the aforementioned end goal in mind?  Asking consent for anything more direct with these family members will probably be a no go right now, due to the estrangement.

replies.content:

You could do a honey pot spell to make yourself as sweet as honey to the relatives in question. This is magic to change you and not them so it does not require their permission. JMG, is there a honey pot spell how to in the FAQ?
Happy Saint Nicholas Day,
Maxine

replies.content: Happy St. Nicholas Day to you as well!  This honey pot spell sounds like it is worth a try.  Anyone have a link to one?

replies.title: Re: Magical Ethics
replies.content: You can find a good general outline here.

replies.title: Re: Magical Ethics
replies.content: Santa Muerte isn’t a Catholic saint, and the Church has repeatedly condemned any devotion to her; even Pope Francis has said as much. I’d recommend St. Joseph’s intercession for the mending of family ties — and, since he’s also the Terror of Demons, for protection from Santa Muerte too!

replies.title: Re: Magical Ethics
replies.content: I am aware of the statements by the Holy Father and some other clerics in the Church. My praxis is clearly outside the mainstream, and I do not think that Santa Muerte is an evil demon that I'll need St. Joseph to chase off for me. This wouldn't be the first devotion that the Church condemned, then later changed her mind about. The Divine Mercy devotion was once condemned, only later to be approved: http://www.angelusonline.org/index.php?article_id=2895&section=articles&subsection=show_article.

replies.title: Re: Magical Ethics
replies.content: You may not think Santa Muerte is a demon at the moment, but hope that you don’t learn better over time. From your link, the Divine Mercy used to be in the same category that Medjugorje is in now; there’s a deep difference between that and a being that will help with literally any intention, including the ones God never grants.

2. Somewhat related to the above question, but another issue for me is that, while I’m seeing plenty of benefits from my practice, I don’t feel like I’ve “made it” yet as a magician. I haven’t had a true “TSW” experience. I haven’t contacted any spiritual entities or done anything fantastic or what have you. I recognize that such things come with time, but it's unclear to me when if ever it will happen. For the time that I’ve invested in this pursuit, it feels that I should be further along. Life is short and I’m impatient to get as much done as possible while I'm here. What do you suggest?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Both these tell me that you're going through one of the reliable events in the life of a mage in training; mystics call it a state of "dryness." When you're in that state, nothing seems to be going anywhere, the benefits you've gotten from your practice don't matter, the goals you have seem hopelessly out of reach, and so on. All you can do in a state of dryness is keep on keeping on -- and after a certain amount of time, you'll realize that it's during these states of dryness that you develop most dramatically as a mage, though you can't perceive that at the time.

Keep on keeping on. When you get the state of dryness, paradoxically enough, that's one of the best signs that you're making good time on the journey.

You might recall that on Wednesday I asked about what the stories we Green Wizards tell ourselves:
I hadn’t considered “Tlön, Uqbar, Orbis Tertius” in conjunction with the conversations we charcoal burners have about the strange origins of the Green Wizards’ Benevolent and Protective Association when we get together with the unicorns to play cards on first and fourth Saturdays…
Perhaps the next thing we should discuss is what our stories about a poor Ruinman who walked away and became the first Green Wizard tell us about ourselves and our world…?
Please advise.
Your response was, “I recommend discussing it with the unicorns!”
So, I did. On Saturday night, I brought the stories up with the usual card not-so-sharps. In Ohio, unicorns are the predatory type – they look like very large, long-snouted mountain lions, (they drink from human cups with reed straws) with long, fluffy, semi-prehensile tails, and iridescent polar bear-like fur. (A tweenaged girl is about their weight limit for a rider, thus THAT legend.) The toes of the unicorns’ forepaws are quite dexterous, and their dew claws are extensible for use as a thumb, so they can shuffle and hold cards. They also like to ante up by tiddlywinking our wooden poker chips with their retractable claws. Any that land in the beer belong to the mug holder.
The unicorns tell me that the stories about the GWB&PA are about rejecting the losses caused by the end of carbon and healing by building a new organization based on the forms tossed aside over the last five centuries. Anti-nostalgia leading to anti-hauntology, if you will.
Then I asked my staves what our stories about a poor Ruinman who walked away and became the first Green Wizard tell us about ourselves and the world.
Ngetal, reversed. A need to heal.
Walking home, I saw a rabbit, so I meditated on the rabbit or hare. When I asked my staves what to notice about this.
Ngetal, upright. “Beginning of heroic deeds, that is, healing.”
Then I picked up “Tlön.” It was open to the last page, and a line jumped out at men. “A scattered dynasty of solitary men has changed the face of the world.”
I think we might be on the right path for the right reasons… but… would you and the commentariat grant me the traditional favor of the fraternity of slide rule users and check my work.
Rhydlyd

comments.title: Request to Stop Book Donations and Library Update
comments.content:
Hi JMG and Ecosophians,

Thanks to everyone who has donated books for my library. Right now the physical library is on hold, so I am asking people to stop donating books, at least for the moment. As many of you might already know, I rented a commercial space for the last 11 years in Naperville, Illinois for my music lesson studio. Originally I envisioned combining the music lesson space with a subscription library. Since 2020, running a small business out of a commercial space has become too expensive to sustain. Though I always seem to maintain a core of approximately 20 loyal students at any given time, 20 students is a far cry from the 40 - 50 needed to pay the bills and clear the most modest of profits. Thanks to my own Ogham divinations and help from fellow Ecosophian and horary astrologer Andrew Skeen, I made an informed decision to collapse my Studio and teach out of my own home in a nearby town. I’ll be making the final part of move by February when my commercial lease expires.

Please pray for me to find a suitable way to make the physical library happen. For now, it is at http://patreon.com/athenareadersclub as an online version. There are lots of great occult and rare books available to patrons in the continental US.

Druidry gave me sufficient antennae over the last six years to discern that renting a commercial space in the existing climate will only serve to exhaust my resources and snuff out my ability to run a library at all. Commercial rents are at all time highs. I sincerely hope it will get easier to rent commercial space in the future. For now, I’m collapsing to avoid the rush in hopes of rising again in a better climate for small-scale entrepreneurs. I am fairly sure that this is the right thing at the right time. Again, thanks everyone for all your help and book donations so far.

replies.content:
My own experience backs that advice up. Banishing is a good way to prep if certain subjects reveal an underlying out-of-balance inner condition from reading or listening to material as it did in my case. I suppose in that sense reading up can be used to give one a clue as to something that needs inner attention to heal. That's how I used it anyway.

There are other things you can pair the banishing with too. I learned to place my hands in namaskar (praying palms) position then invoke in myself a feeling of joy. I would do this whenever I started to have a panic attack or feeling I was being haunted by not-so-kind entities. Actually, whether or not I was actually being attacked by entities ended up not really being the point. It was revealing an unbalanced inner condition I myself was feedback looping into and didn't even realize it. It takes time in unlearning and replacing that circuit loop.

Anyway,

If you place your palms in namaskar it joins the right and left halves of your brain and body (ida & pingala) and slowly brings stability to the system. Pair it with summoning up inside yourself a feeling of joy or pleasantness. I would do it imagining myself standing in a majestic grove of ancient trees with a beautiful sun and gentle breeze and many flowers and butterflies all around me and letting the corresponding feeling of enjoyment and peace stay with me.

I know, in the midst of a panic attack this is something of a hurdle, especially initially. So its good to do this practice when you're not panicky too. Helps train the brain and body and then if you do the practice while panicking it will recognize it and begin the shift. As with any practice you get out of it to the degree of effort you put into it daily.

Sadhguru says if you can hold onto this joyfulness or pleasant feeling for 3-5 minutes it will spread through out the entire pranic body. The goal needs to be at least 3 minutes then ratchet to 5 because spreading such an inner state throughout the body is 100x slower than the time it takes for synapses to transmit. It needs to spread throughout the entire body for long-term benefit. Start with seeing if you can hold onto the pleasantness or joy for 10 seconds then add 1-5 seconds every other day thereafter.


p.s.

You could also add intoning AUM daily. It has huge healing and joy benefits seldom mentioned but its such a beginner-friendly practice most people ignore it because their Ego wants the advanced stuff instead. AUM can be used as the base for attaining to Vak Shuddhi*. That's what Sadhguru has his disciples do. So 2 major benefits (with one being a bonafide superpower) from just one daily beginner practice. Good for time-pressed, busy people.



Hope this helps. :)



*The opposite of what happened to Anneliese's voice.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's an interesting question with a very complex answer. "Daimon" was the ancient Greek word for any spiritual being that was more powerful than humans but not one of the immortal gods. It got turned to "demon" when Christianity arrived and Christians insisted that any spiritual being that wasn't subservient to their god was evil. Later on, in the Romantic era, the term "daimonic" came to be used for the whole range of spiritual influences that could seize human minds -- roughly what Jung meant by "archetypal." That's the sense in which I was using the word.

replies.content:
On 2), not sure if it's helpful, but some thoughts to consider:

a. I tend to like Robert Heinlein's definition of "love" as "Love is that condition in which the happiness of another person is essential to your own.” It seems to pretty well cover the different flavors of love for romantic partners, family, children, friends, and so forth.

b. I find it helpful to look at different kinds of "love" and think about what they have in common. Back in college, a fellow I met asserted that the Greeks better understood love because they had different words for different kinds of love: "eros" for sexual/romantic love, "philadelphia" for brotherly love, and so forth. I had recently been introduced to Owen Barfield's thought that earlier, more "primitive" language was more likel to use one word for many linked concepts than to have specialized vocabulary, due to a recognition of "sameness" - such as "spiritus" meaning "wind", "breath", and "life force" all at once. Barfield proposed the unified meaning was older, and the splitting up into different concepts was later. So I pointed out that while there was value in being able to distinguish between what two friends feel for each other, what two brothers feel for each other, what a mother feels for her child, or a husband for his wife, the fact that English recognizes that all of these feelings are some kind of "love" is perhaps interesting and worthwhile.

c. Some relationships/experiences are going to hit you over the head. Losing my mother made me realize what I had had with her in a way I took for granted while she was still around. Having my daughter has at times overwhelmed me with feeling for her.

d. This one deserves an extra-large grain of salt: Before certain spiritual experiences brought about by following the kind of practices we discuss here, I took a shot with mind-altering substances, and besides some deep insights into my own psychology, I was left with the impression "love is the truth". It just seemed self-evident that kindness, empathy, and a warm recognition of connectedness was the right way to understand the world, and that fear, competition, anger, and so forth were some kind of error or misunderstanding, perhaps building on the errors of others. Here, "love" was a much more abstract, but also perhaps more "cosmic", force

e. Since giving more credence to the kind of things we talk about here, I have also found reincarnation a useful tool for extending "love", or at least empathy. For example, When I see a spider or cockroach or the like, I think about the fact that maybe that's a soul learning what lessons spiderness or cockroachness have to teach, and that in doing what they're doing, there's no ill-will, or if there is, maybe that's something they need to experience. I'm not going to let them crawl all over me or my food, mind you, and I might still squash them if they seem to have a chance of biting me or contaminating my food or something, but it's more thoughtful and less angry than it might once have been.

comments.title: Question Phrasing
comments.content:
Hello and thank you for providing this forum.

Are there best practices for phrasing questions to oracles?

Sometimes when I ask a question in the format "Should I do X?" my tarot cards throw it back at me, as if to say "I don't know, what do you think?" Of course, the question of whether or not I "should" do something depends on a lot of subjective factors: whether I want to; which of the available options I prefer; whether I feel the outcome is worth the cost (effort involved, possible downsides).

On the other hand, asking more specific questions might lead in the wrong direction. I can ask "if I do X, will I get result Y?" The answer could be a clear "yes," but I haven't asked about possible downsides or better options that may be available.

So, what's an open-ended way to ask an oracle for general advice on a particular situation?

replies.title: Re: Question Phrasing
replies.content: It depends very much on the oracle -- and on the diviner. One thing that seems to work well for many people is to start by thinking through all the subjective factors, so you have them in mind as you frame the reading, and can say, "Given x, y, and z, is going ahead with this the best option available to me?"

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The traditional teaching is that when you make an esoteric spiritual tradition public, you get all the karma, good and bad, that results from all the use made of it. So if you turn something over to the public and a lot of evil happens, you get the karma for that, just as much as if you did the evil.

2) Flake filters, obligations, evasive language that requires patience and study to decode -- there are all kinds of gimmicks, and I use most of them. ;-)

3) See above.

One thing that's also in the traditional lore here and there is that we live in an extremely difficult time, when it's necessary to open up magical teachings to the public to a much greater extent than otherwise. The reason for that, as I was taught, is to make sure the traditional lore survives this era of materialism and its aftermath. Only by getting it into many hands is its survival assured. The karmic issue is still there, and those who distribute the teachings know that they're accepting the potential for a significant karmic burden by doing so -- but it needs to be done.

Every action you perform generates consequences, and those consequences are part of your karma. Sometimes that works out in quirky ways, but if you do something you intend to be beneficial and it turns out to cause harm, why, you get the karma of intending something good that ends up going bad. Equally, if you set some nefarious plan in motion and it ends up accidentally helping people, you get the karma from that.

replies.title: Re: Meditation
replies.content:
Mouni Sadhu's own spirituality was esoteric Christian with a big helping of Hinduism on the side, and so yes, he was into the idea that the natural world is an illusion and we need to bail out of it. That ideology doesn't influence his practice as much as you might think! If you want to approach his work as a Druid, do the first three series from Concentration but not the later ones, and the first four series of practices from Meditation, replacing the later ones with your own choice of themes instead of his.

You're not doing anything wrong. Meditation takes time to ripen; if you've got to the point where you can engage in ordinary intense thinking on a theme at will, you've made good progress.

comments.title: after-death process
comments.content:
I have a recently developed interest in the after-death process. Previously I had only a mild and detached curiosity.

A member of my family who had been in declining health for several months, took a turn for the worse a few weeks ago. During these months we spent more time together than usual and deepened our relationship. They went into hospice care on the night of Friday, November 19 and died this past Friday afternoon. I was told of the passing a couple of hours later. I lit a candle for them that night with no special ceremony.

Tonight is well into the third day of the period between the first and second deaths and I don't know what if anything to do, think about, or watch out for. I have some of Dion Fortune's books, but not Through the Gates of Death. I have access to your first Druidry Book and the DMH. The only relevant text from my ancestral tradition that I have much familiarity with is Ecclesiastes.

During the past few months, I sent blessings and love to this person, and made extra offerings to deities that I have relationships with. I continued on with this, feeling a fairly strong connection with my relative, after they went into hospice care. They lingered longer than anyone expected and I finally realized that I should tell them not to stay on my account. Since Thursday, radio silence and no dreams about them, which does not surprise me.

Do you have any advice for me?

replies.title: Re: after-death process
replies.content:
First, please accept my condolences. It's obvious that your family member's death has been a difficult process for you. May you receive the healing and solace you need.

Second, don't worry about it. Death is a natural, healthy process, and your family member is well on their way into the process. The lack of dreams or psychic connection is a good sign that everything went the way it should; if the person had a long illness, the second death could well have happened very quickly, in which case they're safe and sound on the other side. Mourn them, celebrate their life, remember the good memories, and let go.

replies.title: Re: after-death process
replies.content:
First off, my condolences. It really, really sucks to lose a loved one.

Secondly, if I might share a bit as someone who lost a beloved family member after a long illness not so long ago and has been dealing with it (my mother, 3 years ago):

a. The most important thing I had to learn was that "understanding" what had happened was not the same thing as working through it - yeah, sure, you may know that their suffering is over and have accepted that they've moved on, but it still really hurts and really sucks that they're gone and you can't see/talk to them
b. Finding the right ways to talk about and cope with this can be very helpful, whether that's through prayer, journaling, therapy, or whatever else
c. If you have other close family members mourning, it can be tough, especially if anyone involved wants to turn it into "grief olympics" to compete over who is the most distraught. No one's claim on grief is greater than yours, your grief is not diminished or lessened by other friends or family members having different reasons to be sad
d. You might find works dealing with grief helpful, but be careful! I read When Breath Becomes Air after hearing it was a very powerful treatment of grief and dying. I didn't realize it was about someone being diagnosed with Stage IV lung cancer with zero history of smoking, and leaving behind a growing family, all of which was much closer to home than I knew when I started the book. It was good, and I did get value out of it, but if I had known it was going to leave me sobbing, I might not have read it on my lunch break at work

Again, my deepest condolences, and I send plenty of healing and strengthening energy your way,
Jeff

The Church promoted certain saints as replacements for pagan gods. St. Brigit for Brigid is the most famous (she was an ally of St. Patrick, named for the goddess Brigid); St. Maurice for Mars is the most widespread.

All of these saints were chosen for similar names, but also because their lives represented a Christian correction to the pagan god’s way of doing things — sometimes a small one (Roman Mars and St. Maurice would get along well if they ever met), sometimes enormous (St. Veronica or the virgin martyr St. Venera for Venus).

I've been meditating on the numerology of the Qabalah and how it correlates to states of consciousness, mentalities, and perspectives. We seem to be living in a time when the binary is strongly manifesting: you're either with us or against us, red or blue, white or black. In a binary state of consciousness there is often a myopic focus and an obsessive attachment to one pole of the binary that removes all possibility of self-reflection or mutual understanding. Culturally, you can also see this in popular franchises such as Star Wars or Lord of the Rings where the good guys, who are always pure, attractive and lily-white, are fighting the nefarious moustache-twirling bad guys, who are irredeemably evil. The binary mentality, when pushed to the extreme, implies a "means justifies the ends" mentality to destroy the bad guys, removing one pole of the binary, which may even culminate in such things as genocide. The binary is transcended by the ternary, where an awareness of the extremes and a detachment from them is made possible. Accordingly, the numbers and their associated perspectives correspond to the Sephiroth on the Tree of Life, 1 with Kether, 2 with Chokmah, and so on.

1. I'm having trouble extending this logic to the higher numbers, such as four, five, six, and so on, because the complexity seems to rise dramatically. I can't think of any reasonably common situations in which there are, for example, seven sides to an issue or seven different perspectives, at least none which are as satisfying and intuitive as those corresponding to the first three numbers. Do you have any suggestions?

2. I'm assuming that from a Qabalistic point of view, no perspective is "bad" in and of itself, but that all are useful for accomplishing some purpose. If we're trying to fight the Third Reich, for example, a binary mentality might be useful, but it becomes pathological when we identify all of our opponents with the Third Reich. At that point the binary may need the balancing of the ternary. The key is not to look for the "best" perspective, but to recognize which perspective is appropriate to a given situation, as there is no absolute best perspective.

3. Is there a particular Sephirah that corresponds to this meta-level awareness of the relativity of perspectives? I'm thinking Chokmah as Wisdom or Kether as the final unity containing all perspectives might be good candidates, but I'd be curious to hear your thoughts.

2) A binary is useful when you want to lock something into place. Defeating the Third Reich was a ternary process -- the Anglo-American alliance, the Soviet Union, and the Third Reich all contending against each other -- and once the Third Reich was destroyed, the binary that resulted froze up for decades. replies.title: Re: Sublimating the will in cases of Mass obsession?
replies.content: The problem with what you seem to be suggesting is that you don't have access to the will of a mass obsession -- it's still inside the people who are obsessed -- and so you can't sublimate it. We are living through a period of radical simplification and contraction in the natural world, like the hundreds of other periods of the same kind that have existed in the past. On the other side of it will be a new period of diversification and abundance. It so happens that human beings are one of the main forces driving the contraction, but we're far from the first life form to have that role -- the first photosynthetic algae wiped out entire ecosystems of chemosynthetic bacteria by releasing oxygen into the atmosphere, for example.

Human society is the same way. Periods of imperial consolidation alternate with periods of collapse, diversification, and the rise of new cultures. We're in the last years of a period of consolidation with a new dark age on the horizon, and another round of diversification and the birth of new cultures is coming. It's just part of the cycle -- and the spirits and powers of the cosmos know that cycle and shape it to their wills.

comments.title: Pluto
comments.content:
So in the Covid posts there has been a discussion on the role of Pluto in the current affairs of the world. You have expressed the view that Plutos influence on earth is waning, if so what is your perspective on its influence on the worlds current events?

(feel like I need a little microphone or something reporter like to phrase that question)

replies.title: Re: Pluto
replies.content:
Not JMG, but my take on it is:

Pluto is fading out, but "fading out" doesn't mean "gone." There's a spin-down Saturn cycle to go and it's only halfway done.

On that note, it is, in fact, halfway done. The Saturn self-opposition in the demotion chart goes exact on February 10. In a natal chart, this opposition and the nearly simultaneous Uranus self-sextile are associated with the "teenage rebellion" phase of life. Pluto may be "acting out." Worth noting that the 2008 stock market crash happened not long after the first Mars return of the demotion chart - analogous to the "terrible twos," if you will.

Moreover, Pluto is not the sort of planet that will just be willing to "go quietly." It has taken Dylan Thomas to heart and wants to rage, rage against the dying of the light. The Saturn oppositions prior to and following its discovery in 1930 happened either at the cusp of or in the middle of world wars. The Saturn opposition preceding its demotion occurred in 1992, right at the final dissolution of the Soviet empire. If Pluto must go out, it wants to do so with a bang, and it probably wants to "take you with me."

replies.title: Re: Pluto
replies.content:
Other things potentially worth noting regarding Saturn's aspects with the demotion chart:

- Saturn conjoined Pluto's event position on January 26, 2017. I wonder what was happening around that date?

- There is a Saturn-Neptune opposition in the demotion chart, so the Saturn-Saturn opposition is nearly simultaneous with a Saturn-Neptune conjunction. This could be suggestive of some of the direction certain current events have been going.

- Around the end of March 2024, Saturn conjoins Uranus in the demotion chart. I wouldn't be surprised if this corresponds with some sort of loss of certain Plutonian-enabled technology, especially as Pluto proper will have ingressed into Aquarius by this point.

replies.title: Re: Pluto
replies.content:
Well, more broadly, the collapse of the Plutonian themes started a lot earlier than 2006 (ex: the Fall of the USSR and the end of the Cold War), and Plutonian themes kept expanding well after 1930 (ex: nuclear weapons and the Cold War). I wonder if the process isn't started one Saturn return before the key moment in both cases, and ends one Saturn return later, again in both cases.

What this implies though is that Pluto was only at full power for a brief period, roughly 1960-1976, and Ceres may never have fully established itself. Well, once I get The Twilight of Pluto I'll need to look at this a lot closer.

replies.title: Re: Pluto

I'm pleased to say that my book on the subject will be released at next year's spring equinox:
[IMAGE:0]

As Brendhelm pointed out, we're about halfway through the waning of Pluto's influence, as it takes roughly one Saturn cycle of 30 years for a planetary influence to arrive (beginning 30 years before its discovery) or fade out (going on for thirty years after its demotion or disproof). comments.title: Gregorian, Byzantine, chant minutiae

Last week there was a continuing discussion of chant, that I was too late to reply to.

1) I only know about Gregorian chant "manuals" (texts that lay out the precise meanings/emotional implications of different note intervals) from passing references to them. I'd LOVE to get my hands on the real thing! But the western tradition's not my wheelhouse, so I've never followed up on that.

comments.title: cell salts
comments.content:
Haven't seen anyone else mention trying this. Just a heads up that I am testing the cell salt formula suggested by Carey after taking one of the unmentionables: https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/149639.html?thread=19194247#cmt19194247

Nat. mur. (natrium chloratum) = sodium chloride

replies.title: Re: cell salts
replies.content:
Back in the day, when biochemic cell salts were commonly used by occultists and their properties were taught in many occult correspondence courses, it was held that the negative health consequences of mainstream medicine could be treated with a course of Nat. mur. 6x and Kali sulph. 6x, three doses of each every day, in alternation -- that is, a dose of one on rising, a dose of the other a few hours later and so on.

So, yes, you've got the traditional pairing.

Two other combinations could also be considered relevant. Illnesses that caused blood clotting or damaged the epithelial cells lining the blood vessels were treated with Calc. sulph. 6x and Kali mur. 6x, taken together four times daily. When acute inflammatory illnesses occurred, the treatment recommended in those days was Ferrum phos. 6x and Nat. sulph. 6x, taken together four times daily. The usual dose for an adult was four tablets, and of course 6x is a specific homeopathic dilution.

Of course, none of this should be taken as anything approaching medical advice, which I am not legally qualified to preside, and does not claim to treat, heal, or cure any illness or set of illnesses. It is provided purely as a historical curiosity.

comments.title: Druidry Not Based in Golden Dawn Tradition
comments.content:
Dear JMG:

You may have answered this question before. Are there Druid traditions that are not related to the GD or any other systems that include magic. Is it possible to be a Druid mystic and practice no magic.

Trying to figure out what the heck I am.

Much love,
Elizabeth

replies.title: Re: Druidry Not Based in Golden Dawn Tradition
replies.content:
Good heavens, yes. Most Druid traditions active today have nothing to do with the GD and include little or no magic. Mysticism is one very standard option.

Besides, no two Druids do things the same way, so if you want to be a Druid mystic, three hundred years of eccentric Druids are smiling at you and saying, "Do what works for you."

JMG,

comments.title: frustration with sport
comments.content:
I'm trying to get back into surfing bigger waves after major knee surgeries followed by a long recovery where I gained a lot of weight. Using intermittent fasting I'm almost back down to what I was and getting my endurance back but there is still a lot of stiffness all over. I've been doing yoga which points out that most tension in my body is emotional, and my wife says I'm putting too much pressure on myself and should just try to have fun. The problem is even the small waves are very competitive here and if I relax then I don't catch anything which zaps my confidence and I get frustrated and stiff which turns it back into the same cycle.

Today I was out there struggling to get to my feet/getting mowed down by the sets and thinking really hard about the rising and falling triangles of the hexagram which, although did not help me catch more waves, did help me accept defeat more gracefully. When my emotions started going off the rails I remembered the ultimate goal that remains even if I am never able surf again. Sometimes I feel like a spoiled brat with my first world problems, but then realize most of the men in my family had heart attacks so this karma is something I will have to face one way or another. Better to meet it head head on and at least stay in shape while trying.

The question: my will got me this far but it feels like I'm at the point where it's appropriate to ask for assistance. My magic experience includes doing planetary charities and being an Ovate most of the way through the Bardic Grade of CGD. So far I had brief contact with a Sirona and Hu (both happened organically and not for any particular reason), and wondering if it's okay to pray for help, who would be the appropriate god/s, and if there are any rules I should follow regarding making such overtures?

Or if there is something else you have in mind I'm open to that as well

Thanks

replies.title: Re: frustration with sport
replies.content:
First, please be careful. Guys die of heart attacks by pushing themselves too hard in situations like this.

Second, you can certainly invoke either of the deities who've contacted you, or any other -- Druid deities are fairly easygoing that way. The crucial point is to spend at least as much time listening as talking, and be prepared to be told things you don't want to hear.

replies.title: Re: frustration with sport
replies.content: I won't bore you with the details but it went better than expected. Have a positive, productive, and fulfilling week.

comments.title: Help
comments.content: Sorry if this is not appropriate but I feel lost with nowhere to turn. In the last 12 months I have found out about collapse (crushing everything I thought I knew about the world), my husband of 10 years who I love dearly has cheated on me and has decided that he has been comfortable but not happy all this time and my chronic pain (as a result of childhood trauma) which I spent years getting rid of has returned (I had severe attachment issues and your husband cheating on you kinda messes you up). I'm also suffering from a huge bout of existential depression. I'm extremely worried that I'm about to end up in a suicides grave. I'm wondering if there is any advice from everyone here on how to get through this and any spiritual advice? I feel completely alone.

replies.title: Re: Help
replies.content: Please consider getting professional help. You're going through a serious crisis, and I recommend finding a counselor or therapist you can trust who will walk you through the process of coming to terms with what's happened and getting back on your feet.

replies.title: Re: Help
replies.content: If only there was such thing as a collapse aware therapist, who didn't treat you like you were crazy or just going through a 'phase'.

replies.title: Re: Help
replies.content: I hope you'll see this comment, though it is well past midnight.  If a therapist is treating you as crazy or just going through a phase, then get rid of that therapist and find a different one!  I've been through counseling before, with different therapists, and they're trained to take into account their client's worldview and treat them with respect during the course of counseling.  Their job is to help you, not bring your views and beliefs into line with mainstream thinking.  So, I definitely second JMG's suggestion to seek out a counselor.

replies.title: Re: Help
replies.content:
Please do not commit suicide. I think all the industrialized countries have suicide hotlines. Use yours if you need it, and know that you’ll find friends on this site—real friends, who will help in ways tangible and intangible. Please keep posting. We care.

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: Help
replies.content:

You are not alone.
You got us at Magic Monday.
